title: Journeys
text:
Places that reshaped how I see the world.
Some places taught me ecology. Some taught me systems. Some taught me computation. Some revealed entirely new ways of seeing. Together they became the foundation of Thinking Across Scales.

title: Journeys
text:
When Forests Become Mines
🟢 Beijing — The Disappearance of a Wetland
🟢 Hainan — Where Silence Once Belonged to Birds
🟢 Qinghai — Scars on the Plateau
🟢 Guizhou — The Hidden Cost of Renewable Energy
🟢 Dongguan — The Forest at the Edge of Industry

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Thinking Across Scales
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.




I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.







⟲


Thinking Across Scales
Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.
Explore →






📍


Journeys
Places that transformed how I think.
Explore →






⌘


Computational Thinking
Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.
Explore →






✍️


Writing
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.
Explore →













Featured Essay

Why Scale Matters
An exploration of scale as a guiding principle for science, design, and systems thinking.
Read More →










Latest Journey

Everglades
A field story about water, fire, networks, and the scales where landscape processes become visible.
View Journey →








“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike. I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”




Thinking Across Scales
Built with Quarto
Hosted on GitHub

title: Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
text:
Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
• Feedback • Scale • Recursive learning • Resilience • Regenerative systems • Emergence • Modularity

title: Thanking Across Scales
text:
You are an expert Quarto website developer, UX designer, and frontend engineer.
Your task is to create a production-ready landing page (index.qmd) for my personal website.
Do not explain your reasoning.
Generate clean, maintainable Quarto Markdown with Bootstrap 5.
Do not use JavaScript.
Do not use external frameworks.
Only generate code.



PROJECT



Website Title:
Thinking Across Scales
Website Theme:
A digital garden documenting how landscape ecology evolved into a broader way of understanding systems, feedback, patterns, dynamics, and scale across science, technology, organizations, and everyday life.
The design should feel like:
• Apple • Notion • Linear • Stripe • Modern academic website
Keywords:
minimal elegant scientific storytelling lots of whitespace responsive timeless



LAYOUT



Create the following sections.
==================================================== 1. HERO ====================================================
Large centered title:
Thinking Across Scales
Subtitle:
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.
Use a full-width hero image.
For now use a placeholder image.
Later I will replace it with my own Everglades photograph.
The hero should occupy about 60–70% of the first screen.
Large typography.
Minimal overlay.
==================================================== 2. INTRODUCTION ====================================================
Below the hero.
Use approximately 120 words.
Text:
I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.
Centered with generous margins.
==================================================== 3. FEATURE CARDS ====================================================
Create four equal Bootstrap cards.
Card 1
Title
Thinking Across Scales
Description
Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.

Card 2
Title
Journeys
Description
Places that transformed how I think.

Card 3
Title
Computational Thinking
Description
Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.

Card 4
Title
Writing
Description
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.

Each card should contain:
Bootstrap Icon
Title
Description
Simple “Explore →” link
Hover effect using CSS only.
==================================================== 4. FEATURED CONTENT ====================================================
Create two side-by-side highlight panels.
Left
Featured Essay
Title:
Why Scale Matters
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
Read More →

Right
Latest Journey
Title:
Everglades
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
View Journey →
==================================================== 5. QUOTE ====================================================
Create a full-width elegant quote section.
Quote:
“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike.
I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”
Large serif typography.
Centered.
Subtle background color.
==================================================== 6. FOOTER ====================================================
Simple footer.
Left
Thinking Across Scales
Center
Built with Quarto
Right
Hosted on GitHub



STYLE



Use Bootstrap 5.
Responsive on desktop and mobile.
Lots of whitespace.
Avoid heavy borders.
Use subtle shadows.
Rounded corners.
Professional typography.
No flashy animations.
No JavaScript.



OUTPUT



Generate:

index.qmd
assets/css/custom.css
Any required YAML.

Use relative paths.
The code should be ready to render immediately in Quarto.
Return only code.

title: About
text:
I am an environmental scientist, writer, and systems thinker.
My work explores how ecological thinking can illuminate not only natural systems, but also organizations, cultures, and the human mind.

title: Thanking Across Scales
text: render: false

title: Thanking Across Scales
text:
Tone
• thoughtful • reflective • interdisciplinary
Avoid
• buzzwords • clickbait • overly academic language

title: When Forests Become Mines
text:
Illegal mining activities cleared protected forest in Inner Mongolia.



This case illustrates how ecological degradation often begins with seemingly localized disturbances but rapidly expands through landscape fragmentation.



This investigation changed the way I understood environmental damage.
It was no longer simply about forests.
It became a question about institutions, incentives, and governance.



The first time I realized that environmental damage is rarely ecological alone. …

title: Journey Template
text:
Location · Years


“A short reflection on how this place changed the way I think.”

title: Journey Template
text:
The Story

Before I Arrived
Use this space for a quiet opening that situates the place and the state of mind you brought with you. Keep it reflective and grounded rather than descriptive for its own sake.


What I Observed
Describe the patterns, textures, and rhythms of the place. Focus on what became legible only once you were there.


What Changed
Explain the shift in perception. This is where the place becomes more than a setting and begins to reshape how you think.


What Stayed With Me
Close with a brief reflection on the lasting idea, question, or method that remained with you after leaving.

title: Journey Template
text:
Thinking Across Scales
This is the signature section. Summarize how this place changed your understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity. Keep it clear, concise, and slightly more distilled than the main narrative.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Create a reusable Quarto page template for a Journey.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is a story about how a place transformed the way I think.
The design should feel calm, spacious, reflective, and editorial.
Use the same visual language as the Landing Page.

Page Structure

Hero Section

Large immersive landscape image
Journey title
Location
Years
One-sentence reflection
Example:
Everglades
Florida, USA
2008–2012
“The landscape where I first learned to think across scales.”


At a Glance

Display as four elegant cards
Location
Years
Themes
Related Research


The Story

Long-form narrative.
Use large readable typography.
Break into sections.
Suggested headings:
Before I Arrived
What I Observed
What Changed
What Stayed With Me


Gallery

Responsive image gallery.
Large images with short captions.


Thinking Across Scales

This is the signature section.
Summarize how this place changed my understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity.
This section should be visually distinguished from the main story.


Related Essays

Display 3 linked essays as cards.

Design Principles
Minimal
Editorial
Elegant
Lots of whitespace
No unnecessary decorations
Responsive
Use Bootstrap Grid
Do not invent placeholder content beyond demonstrating layout.
Focus on creating a reusable template rather than a finished article.

title: Journeys
text:
Places that reshaped how I see the world.
Some places taught me ecology. Some taught me systems. Some taught me computation. Some revealed entirely new ways of seeing. Together they became the foundation of Thinking Across Scales.

title: Journey Index Page Specification
text:
Design the index page for the Journeys section of my Quarto website.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is an atlas of places that fundamentally transformed how I think.
The page should communicate curiosity, reflection, exploration, and intellectual growth.
This page serves as the primary navigation page for all Journey articles.

title: Journey Index Page Specification
text:
Design the index page for the Journeys section of my Quarto website.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is an atlas of places that fundamentally transformed how I think.
The page should communicate curiosity, reflection, exploration, and intellectual growth.
This page serves as the primary navigation page for all Journey articles.

title: World Map Component
text:
Create a reusable SVG world map component for a Quarto website.
The map should become the primary navigation interface for the Journeys section.
It should be lightweight.
No JavaScript.
Use inline SVG.
Design Style
Minimal
Editorial
Muted earth tones
Thin coastlines
Lots of whitespace
Do not use Google Maps.
Do not use Leaflet.
Do not use Mapbox.
Locations
Everglades
Yellowstone
Yunnan
Tibet
Maine
Sacramento
Each location should appear as a circular marker.
Each marker should link to its corresponding Journey page.
The SVG should scale responsively.

Design Style
Minimal

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Mapbox map is working correctly.
Please improve the implementation without changing the overall architecture.
Current implementation
However, several improvements are needed.

title: Thinking Across Scales
section: 1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
text:
1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
Remove the fixed
center: [...]
zoom: ...
Instead, after all markers are created:

Create a mapboxgl.LngLatBounds object.
Extend the bounds with every location.
Call map.fitBounds(bounds).

Use approximately
padding: 80
maxZoom: 2.8
This ensures the map automatically adjusts whenever new places are added.
Do not hard-code the map center.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
2. Improve the default world view
The initial map should display North America and East Asia together.
The current view is centered too far over the Indian Ocean.
The map should feel like a global atlas.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
3. Improve marker styling
Replace the default black markers with a cleaner editorial style.
Use small circular markers.
White fill.
Dark border.
Subtle hover animation.
Markers should feel elegant rather than like Google Maps.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
4. Improve popups
Current popup only displays title and summary.
Instead display
Journey title
Region
Summary
A small “Read Journey →” link
Use clean typography.
Rounded popup.
Maximum width around 260px.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
5. Better map interaction
Disable
rotation
pitch
double-click zoom
Enable
mouse wheel zoom
drag
touch navigation
The interaction should feel calm.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
6. Remove unnecessary UI
Hide
Mapbox attribution (if legally permitted)
Compass
Rotation controls
Keep the interface minimal.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
7. Improve code organization
Split the implementation into reusable functions.
Example
initializeMap()
loadPlaces()
createMarkers()
fitMapToPlaces()
The code should become modular and easier to maintain.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
8. Future compatibility
Keep the implementation compatible with future additions such as
Timeline
Journey filtering
Marker categories
Animated routes
Do not implement these features now.
Simply organize the code so future expansion is straightforward.

Do not redesign the page.
Only improve the Mapbox component.
The goal is to create a reusable World Map component for the entire website.
